text: The surgeon reduces the herniated stomach from the mediastinum back into the abdomen, revealing the distal body and pylorus. The large hiatal hernia sac is exposed, and a plane is created between the sac and mediastinum by incising the junction with the right diaphragm crus. Blunt dissection is used to separate the sac from the mediastinum, navigating through adhesions. The dissection progresses from six o'clock to twelve o'clock on the right side, reaching the apex of the sac. Once reduced, the anterior vagus, left vagus, and esophagus are visible. The surgeon prepares to dissect posterior to the esophagus to mobilize the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon ties umbilical tape around the gastroesophageal junction, revealing a significant length of the esophagus positioned within the abdomen. The tape provides traction, ensuring the gastroesophageal junction remains stable and does not retract into the thorax.

The surgeon begins preparing the stomach for fundoplication by dividing the greater omentum from the margins of the greater curvature of the stomach. Short gastric vessels are also divided using a harmonic or ligature device. The right vagus nerve, identified as the posterior vagus, is visible lying on the posterior surface of the esophagus near the gastroesophageal junction.

Following fundic mobilization, the surgeon performs the shoe shine maneuver to prevent twisting of the wrap. The hiatal defect is closed using interrupted non-absorbable Ethymond sutures, ensuring the knots are not overly tight to prevent muscle atrophy. The surgeon then carries out a 360-degree floppy Nissen fundoplication, ensuring a 5mm instrument can pass between the wrap and the esophagus.

The completed wrap, consisting of two sutures placed two centimeters apart, covers the distal intra-abdominal part of the esophagus. The wrap is fixed to the GE junction to prevent migration, and the tension-free lower part of the esophagus is visible. The hiatus is repaired using a composite mesh.
